coredata.dc:title: Multichannel sensor array of carbon dots-metal ion pairs for accurate biological thiols analysis and cancer cell discrimination
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Jie
Chen, Jiali
Zhu, Xiaohua
Liu, Meiling
Liu, Yang
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: Biological thiols are essential for mediating physiological and pathological processes. Because of their similarity of structures and reactivity properties, it is a challenging but significant task to identify different types of biological thiols. Herein, a multichannel sensor array was constructed to identify biological thiols based on three-color emitting carbon dots (CDs)-metal ion pairs. Primarily, three metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+, and Fe3+) were selected to quench the fluorescence signals of the three CDs, respectively. Upon the addition of thiols, the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs can be restored or further quenched owing to the synergistic coordinative and steric hindrance effect of the interaction and between thiols, metal ions and the three CDs. Recur to statistical analysis methods, the multichannel sensor array can effectively discriminate six thiols in serum and urine media using only a single “well”. Moreover, the present multichannel sensor array can also be effectively identified cell type from normal or cancer cells. Collectively, the versatile sensor array based on CDs-metal ion pairs offers a highly discerning and adaptive alternative for identifying biological thiols, which exhibit great potential in point-of-care clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biological thiols
Carbon dots
Multichannel sensor array

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discriminate thiols in human biological fluids
Design and fabrication of the multichannel sensor array
Fingerprints of thiols via the multichannel sensor array
Quantitative analysis of GSH by the multichannel sensor array
Mixture identification by the multichannel sensor array
Selectivity of the multichannel sensor array
Detection of thiols in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 2217040228, 22174084, 21904039, 21974042, 21874080 , and 21621003 ) and the Foundation of the Science & Technology Department of Hunan Province ( 2020JJ5352 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence titration curves of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs toward corresponding metals ions (Cu2+, Ag+, and Fe3+) in 10 mM PBS (pH = 5.0).

Discrimination of thiols (5 μM) with the multichannel sensor array. (A) Fingerprints (fluorescence responses) of six thiols, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of thiols to the sensor array, respectively. (B) Heat map derived from the fluorescence response for six thiols. (C) LDA plot for the discrimination of the six thiols. (D) HCA plot for the discrimination of the six thiols.

Sensing property of the multichannel sensor array. (A) LDA score plots of the sensor array responses to different concentrations of GSH. (B) Plot of the first discriminant factor (PC1) and different concentrations of GSH. (C) LDA score plots of the sensor array responses to a mixture of GSH and Cys. Total concentration of the mixture was 5 μM. (D) LDA score plots of the sensor array identification of six thiols in the presence of other interfering substances.

Discrimination of thiols in the real samples. LDA score plots of the sensor array identification of six thiols (all at 5 μM) in human blood serum (A) and urine samples (B). LDA (C) and HCA (D) score plot of normal and cancer cell.

Illustration of the multichannel sensor array for biological thiols and cell recognition. (A) Schematic of the design and fabrication process of the multichannel sensor array. (B) Schematic of the multichannel sensor array recognition of biological thiols in different biological fluids.

head.ce:title: Multichannel sensor array of carbon dots-metal ion pairs for accurate biological thiols analysis and cancer cell discrimination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Beijing Key Laboratory for Analytical Methods and Instrumentation, Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Beijing Key Laboratory for Analytical Methods and Instrumentation, Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, Tsinghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Beijing Key Laboratory for Analytical Methods and Instrumentation, Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biological thiols are essential for mediating physiological and pathological processes. Because of their similarity of structures and reactivity properties, it is a challenging but significant task to identify different types of biological thiols. Herein, a multichannel sensor array was constructed to identify biological thiols based on three-color emitting carbon dots (CDs)-metal ion pairs. Primarily, three metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+, and Fe3+) were selected to quench the fluorescence signals of the three CDs, respectively. Upon the addition of thiols, the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs can be restored or further quenched owing to the synergistic coordinative and steric hindrance effect of the interaction and between thiols, metal ions and the three CDs. Recur to statistical analysis methods, the multichannel sensor array can effectively discriminate six thiols in serum and urine media using only a single “well”. Moreover, the present multichannel sensor array can also be effectively identified cell type from normal or cancer cells. Collectively, the versatile sensor array based on CDs-metal ion pairs offers a highly discerning and adaptive alternative for identifying biological thiols, which exhibit great potential in point-of-care clinical diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiols, especially biological thiols containing cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH), maintain the intracellular redox status and play fundamental roles in physiological processes [1,2]. Particularly, the biological thiols exhibit symbiotic features in metabolic processes. For instance, Hcy is the main precursors for the synthesis of Cys [3], while Cys serves as precursors of GSH [4]. Cys and glutamate are ligated by glutamate-cysteine ligase, generating γ-glutamylcysteine. Next, glycine is added to γ-glutamylcysteine by GSH synthase to form GSH [5]. Thereinto, Cys is a rate-limiting substrate for the synthesis of GSH [6]. At the same time, GSH can also transform to Cys when the Cys deficient [7]. Consequently, aberrant levels of intracellular one biological thiol could impact others, which may cause serious health issues [8]. For example, abnormal levels of these biological thiols are closely associated with a variety of health disorders, such as cancer [9], Alzheimer’s diseases [10], heart diseases [11], liver disease [12], coronary artery diseases [13], and AIDS [14]. Consequently, a simple but effective strategy to identify and determine the biological thiols is prerequisite for understanding their biological functions and transition for clinic diagnosis.

Among the analytical methods for biological thiols, such as chromatography [15], mass spectrometric [16], electrochemistry [17,18], chemiluminescence [19], colorimetry [20,21], and fluorescence spectroscopy [22,23], the optical sensing approach has attracted extensive attention because of their high sensitivity and in situ monitoring ability [24–26]. Up to now, a large number of fluorescent/colorimetric sensors have been developed to sensitively and specifically detect biological thiols [27,28]. However, discrimination of the biological thiols from each other remains a grand challenge due to their similarities in structures and properties.

Fortunately, pattern recognition technologies and sensor array methods provide an ideal platform for detecting and identifying various thiols or mixtures. Array-based sensors, inspired by mammalian noses, consist of different nonselective receptors [29]. The diverse analytes can be recognized through differential receptor-analyte binding interactions [30,31]. The sensor array can efficiently differentiate analytes with similar structures through smart cooperation of versatile sensing systems [32]. In the past decade, various nanomaterials e.g., lanthanide-derived, multiparameters, and persistent luminous receptors have been widely employed as excellent array-based sensing elements for detecting biological thiols [33–37]. Thereinto, carbon dots (CDs) have received significant attention in sensor array development because of their excellent optical properties, facile preparation, and abundant surface functionalization [38,39]. For example, Zhang’s group employed three amino acids derived blue-color emitting CDs-Ag+ ensembles for the identification of biological thiols [40]. Chen and co-workers used one kind of CDs combined with different metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, and Pb2+) for the successful discrimination of biological thiols [41]. These results demonstrated the excellent prospect of CDs in the applications of biological thiols and clinic analysis. Generally, the strategies for thiols analysis are mostly single channel signal output on a multiple array signal input. Thus, the output signals need to be measured in each channel individually, and only one signal response is obtained in one channel for the analytes. Alternatively, the multiplexed sensor devices platform, combining different recognition elements in single-well, which can provide different information and multiple color channels [42,43]. This strategy provided high-throughput information from only one sensor unit, which can effectively reduce the test time and sample volume. Thus, it is still importance and highly desirable to establish a multichannel sensor array for simple, sensitive, and cost-effective detection of biological thiols simultaneously.

Herein, we proposed a multichannel sensor array for the discrimination of biological thiols using CDs-metal ion pairs in a single well. In this work, three representative CDs with independent emission and different functional groups on their surface were rationally fabricated. The metal ions, utilized as both recognition elements and fluorescence quenchers, can efficiently quench the fluorescence signals of the three CDs through chelation, respectively. In the presence of thiols, the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs recovered or further quenched in different degrees owing to the synergistic coordinative effect between thiols, metal ions and the three CDs, giving rise to fingerprint patterns for each thiol ( Scheme 1 A). Furthermore, the multichannel sensing array can be effective identified biological thiols both in the human blood serum and urine samples. Moreover, tumor cells can also be discriminated by the multichannel sensor array through biological thiols that produce in the cytoplasm during the metabolic process (Scheme 1 B). Such a multichannel sensor array strategy exhibits great promise in clinical analysis and cancer diagnostics.

L-glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), dithiothreitol (DTT), thioglycolic acid (TGA), β-mercaptoethanol (ME), thrombin, bovine serum albumin, glucose, urea, ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid were purchased from Energy Chemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glycine, tryptophan, proline and aspartic acid were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. KNO3, NaNO3, Mg(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, AgNO3, and CuSO4 were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent company (Shanghai, China).
The three CDs (blue-emitting CDs: B-CDs, yellow-emitting CDs: Y-CDs, and red-emitting CDs: R-CDs) were prepared according to previous reports [44–46] and are detailed in the Supporting Information.

In the fluorescence quenching experiment, different CDs solution (B-CDs 10 µg/mL, Y-CDs 10 µg/mL, and R-CDs 8 µg/mL) was diluted using the PBS (10 mM, pH=5.0). After that, various concentrations of metal ions (Cu2+ for B-CDs, Ag+ for Y-CDs, and Fe3+ for R-CDs) were added to the CDs solution. Fluorescence spectra of the mixture of CDs-metal ions were carried out through QM40 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PTI, Canada) at room temperature, and the excitation wavelengths were 365 nm, 400 nm, and 500 nm for B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively.

In a 384-well plate, 45 µL CDs diluted with 10 mM PBS were pipetted into each well. The concentration of CDs was 3.3 µg/mL (B-CDs), 3.3 µg/mL (Y-CDs) and 2.7 µg/mL (R-CDs), respectively. Then the corresponding concentration of metal ions (Cu2+, 16 μM; Ag+, 21 μM; Fe3+, 66 μM) were added. After mixing well, 10 µL of thiols solution were added and the final concentration of thiols was 5 µM. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was recorded on Biotek microplate reader and collected at three emission channels (SynergyMX, America). The excitation and emission wavelength were 365/450 nm, 400/500 nm, and 500/620 nm for B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. The data analysis was performed using linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and Hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) in Anaconda Prompt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discriminate thiols in human biological fluids
ce:section.ce:para: The human blood serum samples were first pretreated to remove the protein. Acetonitrile (300 µL) was added into the serum sample (100 µL), and the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. Before testing urine samples, the urine was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. After that, the serum and urine samples were added into the above-constructed arrays to get 5% (v/v) serum and urine media. Then, six kinds of thiol samples (5 µM) were added and tested using the multichannel sensing method.

HepG2 (human hepatoma cells) and HCT116 (human colorectal cells) were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium, Hyclone). LO2 (human normal liver cells), C2C12 (mouse skeletal muscle myoblasts), and A549 (human lung adenocarcinoma epithelial cell) were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium (Hyclone). All the cell culture media contain 10% v/v fetal bovine serum (Gibco) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (Hyclone) in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator at 37 oC. The cell lysates were prepared as described in the Supporting Information. The cell discrimination process was similar to the discriminate thiols in the human biological fluids procedure.

The three-color CDs for developing the multichannel sensor array were prepared according to previously described procedures with minor modifications [44–46]. The morphologies, optical properties, and surface functional groups of the three CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The TEM results indicated that the three CDs are all monodispersed with spherical morphology, and the average particle size is 12.4 ± 2.4 nm, 11.1 ± 1.8 nm, and 7.2 ± 1.0 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The UV–vis spectra indicated that all the three CDs have abundant π-π * and n-π * conjugates (Fig. S2). The fluorescence spectra of the three CDs is virtually independent of the excitation wavelength, which can be obtain independent responses from each emission channel (Fig. S3). Specifically, based on the FTIR and XPS results (Fig. S4, S5), the three CDs have plentiful but different functional groups on their surface, making the three CDs possible to chelate with different metal ions [45,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of the multichannel sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To construct the multichannel fluorescent sensor array for biological thiols sensing, CDs were chosen as the signal reporting units due to their excellent optical properties and possessing plentiful functional groups on their surface. Three CDs with different maximum emission peak position (B-CDs, λmax = 450 nm; Y-CDs, λmax = 550 nm; and R-CDs, λmax = 620 nm) were utilized as signal output. The CDs were chosen based on the following rules: 1) the CDs display least spectral interference with separated emission wavelengths, yielding independent responses from each channel; 2) the CDs containing plenty but different functional groups and exhibit different binding abilities with metal ions; 3) the photostability of the CDs provides reliable fluorescence outputs.

It has been reported that the metal ions could selectively quench the fluorescence of CDs. Given the potential toxicity and the competing interactions of metal ions with the CDs and thiols, three metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+, and Fe3+) were chosen for fabricating the multichannel sensor array. First, the fluorescence titration experiments of metal ions, including Cu2+, Ag+, and Fe3+, against the B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were conducted, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1, the fluorescence intensity of the three-color CDs was decreased pronouncedly as the concentration of metal ions increased, indicating that the metal ions can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the CDs correspondingly. Besides, each CD harbors its own corresponding metal ions to quench the fluorescence, even when exposed to other metal ions, having almost no effect on their fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6). In addition, the association constant (Ka) of three CDs and metal ions (B-CDs/Cu2+, Y-CDs/Ag+, and R-CDs/Fe3+) are 3.5 × 105 M-1, 5.5 × 104 M-1, and 7.3 × 105 M-1, respectively ( Fig. S7). The result indicated that the CDs-metal ions pairs could generate differential fluorescence responses upon the addition of competing analytes, providing the differential affinity required for a multichannel output. To obtain an effective sensitivity and sensing performance, 16 μM Cu2+, 21 μM Ag+, 66 μM Fe3+, were chosen in the following investigation.

Upon the addition of thiols, the fluorescence intensity of the three CDs could be restored or further quenched owing to the different interactions between thiols and CDs-metal ion pairs (Fig. S8-S10). On account of the different functional groups and steric hindrance effect of thiols, the interactions between each thiol and metal ions could be identified. The characteristics of the thiols studied in this sensing platform are shown in (Table S1). In addition, the binding constant of each thiol and metal ions exhibit a definite difference (Table S2). Therefore, the differential fluorescence responses are observed, which can be employed for fabricating a cross-reactive sensor array for identifying thiols. It should be noted that the multichannel fluorescent sensor array is simple and inexpensive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fingerprints of thiols via the multichannel sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the three CDs-metal ion pairs (B-CDs/Cu2+, Y-CDs/Ag+, and R-CDs/Fe3+ pairs) were employed to fabricate the multichannel sensor array. The fluorescence responses of the multichannel sensor array toward different thiols were investigated. The presence of six thiols led to differential fluorescence responses due to their interactions with CDs-metal ions pairs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a characteristic fluorescence response pattern can be observed corresponding to different thiols (5 μM), suggesting differential interactions of the CDs-metal ion pairs with the thiol. By contrast, all the six thiols have negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of three pure CDs (Fig. S11), confirming the metal ions play a major role in the fluorescence response. Moreover, the distinctive response patterns of each thiol can be more easily recognized in the heat map (Fig. 2 B).

Then, the fluorescence responses were evaluated by LDA, and the response patterns of thiols (5 μM) were clustered into six non-overlapping clusters (Fig. 2 C), demonstrating the successful discrimination of the six thiols. Furthermore, the response data matrix was also evaluated by HCA. As shown in Fig. 2 D, each kind of thiols can be effectively classified into different branches of a cluster dendrogram, displaying clear discrimination of the multichannel sensor array. Moreover, the as-fabricated sensor array can also be employed to discriminate thiols at different concentrations (10 μM and 20 μM) (Fig. S12). All the results indicated that our array is a powerful tool for the identification of thiols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of GSH by the multichannel sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To further test the resolving capacity of the multichannel sensor array, different concentrations of GSH were carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 A, GSH at different concentrations can be well distinguished on a 2D canonical score plot. Moreover, the values of factor 1 are gradually reduced as the concentration of GSH increase from 0.5 to 40 μM. Furthermore, the values of factor 1 and the concentrations of GSH exhibiting a well linear relationships in the range of 2–20 μM (Fig. 3 B). According to the standard 3σ criteria [38], the limit of recognition of the sensor array can be calculated to be 0.05 μM, which provided a desirably linear range and detection compared with the previously reported method (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mixture identification by the multichannel sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further evaluate the discrimination detection power of the multichannel sensor array, the discriminability of different molar ratios of GSH and Cys (GSH/Cys = 100/0, 75/25, 50/50, 25/75, 0/100 with 5.0 μM total biological thiol) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 C, the mixtures and pure GSH and Cys can be well discriminated in LDA without any overlap. Moreover, other multi-classification results have also been investigated (Fig. S13). The results demonstrated that the sensor array had a good discriminant ability in thiol mixtures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the multichannel sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the selectivity capability of the multichannel sensor array was also investigated. Several potential interfering compounds that may exist in biological sample including normal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, and Mg2+; 10 mM), small biomolecules (glucose, urea, ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid; 5 μM), amino acid (glycine, tryptophan, proline, and aspartic acid; 5 μM), and protein (bovine serum albumin and thrombin; 5 μM) was monitored. As shown in Fig. 3 D and Fig. S14, although the interfering substances generate responses, the clusters are well separated from the biological thiols. The results indicated that our multichannel sensor array exhibits excellent selectivity toward these biological thiols, suggesting the proposed method could be employed for selectively discriminating biological thiols in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of thiols in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the possible applicability of the multichannel sensor array, blind samples were carried out. Thirty-three blind samples (31 were used as challenges and 2 were blanks) were predicted by the sensor array. The fluorescence recognition patterns of the analytes were measured (Fig. S15), and LDA analysis was operated. As a result, 32 of the 33 samples were correctly recognized with only 3.03% false positives (Table S4). The result indicated that the multichannel sensor array exhibited well accuracy and reliability for discriminating biological thiols.

The concentration of biological thiols interrelates closely with health disorders. Therefore, the discrimination of biological thiols in human biological fluids using the multichannel sensor array was explored. The thiols were spiked in human blood serum (5% v/v) and urine (5% v/v) samples at a concentration of 5 μM, and the fluorescence responses of the sensor array were recorded. LDA was further applied to the data matrixes. The plus thiols and the biological fluids themselves could be effectively discriminated ( Fig. 4 A, B). The results validated that the proposed assay multichannel sensor array exhibits well anti-interference capability and reveals potential applications for identification of thiols in point-of-care clinical diagnosis.

The level of biological thiols has been regarded as an important physiological parameter for cancer diagnosis, and the expression of biological thiols (especially the GSH) in cancer cell is generally higher than that of normal cell [48,49]. Here, the accurate discrimination of cancer cells from the normal cells were investigated using LO2 cells (human normal liver cell) and HepG2 cell (human hepatoma cells) as a model. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the LO2 and HepG2 are classified into two separate regions without overlapping parts, indicating that the cancerous cells can be well identified from normal cells. Moreover, the capacity of the multichannel sensor array to discriminate other normal cells and cancer cells was also examined using a normal cell lines (C2Cl2) and two cancer cell lines (A549 and HCT116). Complete differentiation was also realized between each of the different cell. It should be noted that all the normal cells and cancer cells are classified into two separate regions without overlapping parts (Fig. 4 C). Furthermore, the response patterns were also analyzed by HCA. As shown in Fig. 4 D, the five cell lines are primarily divided into two clusters (i.e., normal cells and cancer cells). More importantly, each cell line could be further divided into different subgroups in the HCA dendrogram. Furthermore, to evaluate the reliability of the sensor array, the total GSH content of the five types cells was also quantified using commercial GSH assay kit. The GSH content of normal cells is lower than that of cancer cells (Table S5). The results indicated that the multichannel sensor array exhibited powerful potential in diagnostic applications.

In summary, a simple but effective multichannel sensor array based on CDs-metal ion pairs was developed to determine and discriminate biological thiols. The proposed multichannel sensor array could be employed for distinguishing and detecting six thiols even at a low concentration of 5 μM. Besides, the sensor array could identify thiols at different concentrations and biological thiols mixtures. The sensor array also exhibited good anti-interference and rapid detection capacity, which could be successfully discriminated individual thiols in the human blood serum and urine samples. Moreover, different cell types could also be discriminated by using the sensor array. Based on their excellent performance, the CDs-metal ions pairs could be fabricated sensor arrays for identifying and sensing other similar analytes, showing great promise in clinical diagnostic and specific biological purposes.

Jie Gao: Experiments, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jiali Chen: Experiments, Discussion. Xiaohua Zhu: Supervision, Manuscript writing. Meiling Liu: Data curation, Discussion. Yang Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Youyu Zhang: Conceptualization, Discussion, Reviewing. Shouzhuo Yao: Conceptualization, Discussion, Reviewing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 2217040228, 22174084, 21904039, 21974042, 21874080, and 21621003) and the Foundation of the Science & Technology Department of Hunan Province (2020JJ5352).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131119.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous fluorescence sensing of Cys and GSH from different emission channels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, Y.-Q. Sun, Y. Huo, H. Zhang, L. Wang, P. Zhang, D. Song, Y. Shi, W. Guo, Simultaneous fluorescence sensing of Cys and GSH from different emission channels, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 136 (2014) 574–577.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.V. Mishanina, M. Libiad, R. Banerjee, Biogenesis of reactive sulfur species for signaling by hydrogen sulfide oxidation pathways, Nat. Chem. Biol. 11 (2015) 457–464.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous visualization of endogenous homocysteine, cysteine, glutathione, and their transformation through different fluorescence channels

Yao, Simultaneous visualization of endogenous homocysteine, cysteine, glutathione, and their transformation through different fluorescence channels, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58 (2019) 4557–4561.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the metallothionein, glutathione and cysteine relationship in rat liver

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hidalgo, J.S. Garvey, A. Armario, On the metallothionein, glutathione and cysteine relationship in rat liver, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 255 (1990) 554–564.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-canonical glutamate-cysteine ligase activity protects against ferroptosis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.P. Kang, A. Mockabee-Macias, C. Jiang, A. Falzone, N. Prieto-Farigua, E. Stone, I.S. Harris, G.M. DeNicola, Non-canonical glutamate-cysteine ligase activity protects against ferroptosis, Cell Metabol 33 (2021) 174–189.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous discrimination of cysteine, homocysteine, glutathione, and H2S in living cells through a multisignal combination strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, L. Xu, W. Chen, J. Huang, C. Huang, J. Sheng, X. Song, Simultaneous discrimination of cysteine, homocysteine, glutathione, and H2S in living cells through a multisignal combination strategy, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 1904–1911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dispersion-dominated chromogenic strategy for colorimetric sensing of glutathione at the nanomolar level using gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xianyu, Y. Xie, N. Wang, Z. Wang, X. Jiang, A dispersion-dominated chromogenic strategy for colorimetric sensing of glutathione at the nanomolar level using gold nanoparticles, Small 11 (2015) 5510–5514.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasma homocysteine as a risk factor for dementia and Alzheimer's disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Seshadri, A. Beiser, J. Selhub, P.F. Jacques, I.H. Rosenberg, R.B. D'Agostino, P.W.F. Wilson, P.A. Wolf, Plasma homocysteine as a risk factor for dementia and Alzheimer's disease, N. Engl. J. Med. 346 (2002) 476–483.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhu, R. Tang, S. Wang, X. Chen, J. Hu, C. Lei, Y. Huang, H. Wang, Z. Nie, S. Yao, Protein@inorganic nanodumpling system for high-loading protein delivery with activatable fluorescence and magnetic resonance bimodal imaging capabilities, ACS Nano 14 (2020) 2172–2182.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jie Gao is studying for doctor degree in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Hunan Normal University. Her current research interest is the optical analysis and chemical biosensor.
Jiali Chen is an undergraduate of Hunan Normal University. She studied optical analysis and chemical biosensor.

Xiaohua Zhu received Ph.D. from State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University. He is a lecturer in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, China. His research is focused on chemical and biological sensing.

Meiling Liu received her PhD degree in Hunan Normal University in 2012. She is a professor in Hunan Normal University since 2018. During 2017–2018, she worked as a visiting scholar at University of California, Los Angeles. Her research mainly focuses on sensing and catalysis, the synthesis and surface engineering of nanomaterials and the preparation of biosensing nanoplatforms for biomarkers.

Yang Liu received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China in 2006. After post-doctoral experience at the University of California at Riverside and Oakland University, he joined the Chemistry Department of Tsinghua University in 2009. His research interests focus on electroanalytical chemistry, nanomaterial electrochemistry and biosensors.

Youyu Zhang received her PhD degree in Hunan University in 2004. She is a professor in Hunan Normal University since 2005. She worked as a visiting scholar at Hong Kong University from 2002 to 2004, at Northwest National Laboratory in the United States from 2008 to 2010. Her research mainly focuses on fluorescence probes and biosensing, preparation and application of group IV nanomaterials and upconversion nanomaterials, photoelectrochemistry and so on.

Shouzhuo Yao is a Professor in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University and he is an academician of Chinese Academy of Science as well.
